1.4 Related work
Integer linear programmingWe give a high-level overview of algorithmic development for
ILP . For a thorough treatment of the field, we refer the reader to the classic texts [GLS88, WN99,
Sch87, CCZ14]. GomoryÔÇÖs cutting plane method was the first algorithm for solving (mixed)
integer programming problems [Gom58]. Papadimitriou [Pap81] proved that ILP was inNP,
and as a by product, gave a pseudopolynomial-time dynamic programming algorithm for ILPs
with a fixed number of constraints.
A significant breakthrough was made by Lenstra [LJ83], who showed that ILPs are polyno-
mial time solvable when the number of variables is fixed. A careful complexity analysis shows
that LenstraÔÇÖs algorithm runs in time 2 O(n2) times a polynomial in the encoding sizeLof the
problem data. Kannan improved the leading factor ton O(n) shortly after [Kan83]. Both al-
gorithms fundamentally rely on lattice point enumeration techniques.Lenstra-typealgorithms
utilize a thinnest direction to decompose the IP into lower dimensional subproblems which
correspond to parallel lattice hyperplanes.Kannan-type algorithmsgeneralize Lenstra-type al-
gorithms by decomposing the feasible region along lattice shifts of a linear subspace.
Building on these ideas, Dadush [Dad12] developed an improved Kannan-type algorithm
for the more general problem ofconvex integer programming, where the task is to decide whether
a convex setK ÔŐćRn contains an integer point. In his thesis, Dadush showed that his algorithm
runs in time 2O(n) nn ┬Ěpoly(L). He further observed that, assuming a conjecture of Kannan and
Lov┬┤asz [KL88] on the best volume-based lower bound for the covering radius of a convex
body with respect to a lattice 4, the overall complexity could be tightened to(log(2n)) O(n) ┬Ě
poly(L). In a recent breakthrough, Reis and Rothvoss [RR23] gave a constructive resolution
of thesubspace flatness conjecture, thereby enabling DadushÔÇÖs algorithm to attain this improved
bound.
The running time of the algorithm described in [RR23] cannot be reduced in general, and
whether there exists a single exponential time algorithm for exact ILP remains open. 5 Alter-
natively, Dadush, Eisenbrand and Rothvoss [DER25] give an algorithm based on reducing the
4Intuitively, the covering radius is the smallest scaling of a convex body that guarantees a lattice point in every
translate.
5Kanann and Lova┬┤sz [KL88] give an instance that saturates the log(n)lower bound for the covering radius.
10
exact ILP problem to a sequence of approximate ones. These intermediate problems are solved
using an algorithm for approximate IP from Dadush [Dad14], which runs in time 2 O(n) . This
approach leads to a framework for exactly solving general ILPs in time 2O(n) ┬Ěn n, but matches
the state of the art(log(2n)) O(n) complexity bound for problems with solutions whose entries
are at most poly(n).
Quantum algorithms for integer and combinatorial optimizationBy now quantum propos-
als for combinatorial optimization have been investigated for over two decades. The Quantum
adiabatic algorithm [FGGS00] and Quantum Approximate Optimization algorithm [FGG14]
are the most thoroughly studied frameworks.
There are also universal quantum speedups for backtracking [Mon18, AK17], which them-
selves furnish universal quantum speedups for branch-and-bound [Mon20, CMYP22]. How-
ever, the speedup here is quadratic. More recently, Decoded Quantum Interferometry [JSW+25]
has been proposed for solving optimization problems over finite fields. This approach is based
on RegevÔÇÖs reduction [Reg09], and obtains exponential speedups for approximately solving
theoptimal polynomial intersectionproblem.
The group relaxation and the corner polyhedronGomory introduced and studied the group
relaxation and the corner polyhedron in [Gom65, Gom67, Gom69]. Wolsey [Wol71] general-
ized the group relaxation tomixed integer programmingshortly after, and introducedextended
relations. Extended relations are all of the possible group relaxations one can get upon drop-
ping successively fewer nonnegativity constraints. One of the extended relaxations is guaran-
teed to solve the ILP to optimality, since the original ILP trivially corresponds to the extended
relaxation obtained from dropping no non-negativity constraints.
There are several studies that investigate strategies for mitigating poor performance in the
degenerate setting [BS77, GNS73, Wol73, WN99]. Empirical studies on the tightness of the
group relaxation and the closely relatedcorner relaxationare provided in [FM08, CMN12].
In addition to characterizing the nondegeneracy condition, Lasserre [Las04] showed that
group relaxations are related to a generalized residue formula from Brion and Vergne [BV97],
which serves as the foundation for efficient lattice point enumeration in polytopes when the
dimension is a fixed constant [Bar94, BP99, DLHTY04].
Paper organization
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 establishes notation and reviews the
necessary background material, covering finite abelian groups, lattices, Markov chains, linear
programming and the generalized shortÔÇôpath framework from [CHO+24]. In Section 3, we de-
velop the null space formulation of the group relaxation, and present a classical MarkovÔÇôchain
local search over the kernel. In Section 4, we give the quantum algorithm: we specify state
preparation and blockÔÇôencodings, and analyze two mixers (productÔÇôofÔÇôcycles and expander
walks) to obtain our main runtime guarantees. Section 5 reports empirical results on applying
the group relaxation to synthetic cuttingÔÇôstock instances and every pure ILP found in MI-
PLIB 2017 [GHG +21]. Appendix A details practical strategies for optimizing the null space
computations, and Appendix B discusses an alternative speedup avenue (Gibbs sampling)
and how this setting interacts with our assumptions.

2.4 The generalized short path algorithm
Short path (SP) algorithms, first proposed by Hastings [Has18] and later refined by Dalzell et
al. [DPCB23], represent a methodological advancement over the QAA [FGGS00]. Below, we
outline the core concepts.
While quantum computers cannot efficiently prepare the ground state for general Hamil-
tonians, certain Hamiltonians have ground states that are easy to construct. The QAA lever-
ages this by starting with an initial HamiltonianH 0 whose ground state is simple to pre-
pareÔÇöcommonly, the transverse-field operatorH 0 =Ôłĺ ÔłĹiÔłł[n] ¤âx
i , where¤â x
i is the PauliXoper-
ator on qubitiÔłł[n]. The algorithm then defines a time-dependent Hamiltonian
HQAA(t) := (1Ôłĺ¤ä(t))H 0 +¤ä(t)H,
where¤ä:[0,T]Ôćĺ[0, 1]is a smooth, monotonic function satisfying¤ä(0) =0 and¤ä(T) =1 so
thatH QAA(0) =H0 andH QAA(T) =H. According to theadiabatic theorem[Mes58], simulating
the Schr┬Ęodinger equation
i d
dt |x(t)Ôčę=H QAA(t)|x(t)Ôčę
for sufficiently largeTprepares the ground state ofH, provided that the minimum gap╬┤ min
between the lowest two eigenvalues is nonzero. Here
╬┤min :=min
tÔłł[0,T]
{╬╗2(HQAA(t))Ôłĺ╬╗ 1(HQAA(t))},
where╬╗ 1 and╬╗ 2 are the smallest and second smallest eigenvalues, respectively.
The challenge is that╬┤min tends to shrink rapidly with system size, necessitating prohibitive
simulation timesT. Roughly,Tscales polynomially with the inverse gap. Obtaining analyti-
cal bounds on the spectral gap is notoriously difficult [HS12], but more importantly, the QAA
is hampered by ÔÇťlocalizationÔÇŁ phenomenaÔÇöquantum analogues of a classical particle getting
trapped in a local minimum. Localization can induce (super) exponentially small gaps, result-
ing in run times that may be exponentially slower than classical brute-force search [AKR10].
SP algorithms avoid first-order phase transitions via two key modifications:
1. Instead of using the standard adiabatic HamiltonianH QAA, the algorithm uses a slightly
different interpolation between the mixer HamiltonianH 0 and the final HamiltonianH
which encodes a truncated and normalized cost function.
15
2. The algorithm does not follow the adiabatic path, but performs two successive jumps
called short jump and long jump to avoid the points where the spectral gap of the Hamil-
tonian decays (super) exponentially.
Thegeneralizedquantum SP algorithm, as developed in [CHO +24], further provides a prin-
cipled framework for achieving super-quadratic quantum speedups over not just over unstruc-
tured search, but also over classical Markov Chain search in combinatorial optimization. The
key ingredient is a generalized mixer Hamiltonian over the Markov Chain graph of interest.
Below, we review the algorithm and conditions for super-quadratic speedups.

In this section, we first review important details regarding the group relaxation in order to
derive a formulation of this problem amenable to local search. We then show how to solve the
reformulated problem usingMarkov chain search, and analyze the complexity of the resulting
scheme.

Lasserre later established anondegeneracy condition:
Definition 7(Nondegeneracy condition for group relaxations [Las04]).Denote the set of all op-
timal bases to the LP relaxation of(ILP)byB. LetB ÔłłBbe an optimal LP basis. If there exists
another basisB ÔÇ▓ ÔłłBsuch thatOPT B =OPT BÔÇ▓ then the problem is degenerate. Otherwise, the group
relaxation defined byBis nondegenerate, and solves(ILP)to optimality.
This nondegeneracy condition can be viewed as a similar statement to the usual notion of non-
degeneracy in linear programming, in the following sense: the situation in which the group
relaxation only provides a lower bound onOPTarises, necessarily, when there is another LP
basisB ÔÇ▓ whose group relaxation yields the same lower bound. We emphasize that group non-
degeneracy is strictly stronger than LP nondegeneracy. Moreover, while LP nondegeneracy
can often be certified a posteriori (e.g., via reduced costs and basic variable signs), no obvious
efficient a posteriori certificate is available for the group case. That said, there is a simplesuf-
ficienta posteriori check for exactness: if a group-optimal solution is ILP-feasible (i.e., satisfies
the dropped nonnegativity), thenOPT B =OPT; conversely, failure of this check does not cer-
tify degeneracy, since there may be another basis which induces a group relaxation that solves
the ILP .

3.4 Markov-chain search algorithms for the group relaxation
In this section, we design a random-walk-based approach for sampling optimal solutions of
the group relaxation. We will show how to construct a random walk over a coset correspond-
ing to the set of feasible solutions, enabling one to run Markov chain search to solve the group
relaxation. The motivation for considering Markov-chain search is primarily for analysis: it
is easier to demonstrate quantum speedup in this framework compared with a classical algo-
rithm that directly samples uniformly over the feasible coset.
As done in the previous section, by exploiting the algebraic structure of the group relax-
ation, one can efficiently generate a basis for the kernel of the linear map defined by the coeffi-
cient matrix with the range mapped toR. Hence, provided any feasible solution to the group
relaxation, we can determine the optimal solution by solving a problem over the null space.
An efficient procedure for obtaining a generating set forKbuys us nothing on its own.
It does however enable one to construct a Markov Chain that explores the feasible region
of the group relaxation, and mixes to a stationary distribution¤Çin poly(n)steps. Letting

4 Quantum short path algorithm for the group relaxation
In this section we apply the quantum short path algorithm to the group relaxation. We provide
two distinct instantiations of the framework. The first relies on the product-of-cycles walk
(defined in Section 3.4) and uses Theorem 2, while the second constructs an expander from the
product-of-cycles walk and applies Theorem 3. Before devling into these algorithms, we show
that the quantum input assumption (Assumption 1) is satisfied for both walks.

The following result shows that there does exist a family of ILPs whose group relaxations
satisfy the conditions for super-quadratic speedups using the product-cycle walk. The follow-
ing ILPs turn out to have a specific structure that enables them to be solved easily. However,
the goal is simply to use this construction to show that our conditions are not vacuous.

4.3 Expander walk approach
We can convert the product-of-cycles walk into an expander graph, with high probability, by
randomly sampling additional elements fromK. Specifically, if we use the minimal generating
setSfound using Algorithm 2 to sampleClog|K|(for some constantC>0) elements eSuni-
formly at random fromK, then with high-probability both eSwill generateKand the Cayley
graph spanned by eSis an expander (Algorithm 5). The latter part follows from theAlonÔÇô
Roichman theorem[AR94], which asserts that the spectral gap is╬┤Ôëą¤Áfor a constant¤Á>0.
Since the Cayley walk that we generate is with high probability a spectral expander, we refer
to this approach as theExpander Walk.

j=1)

j=1), compute|K|.

5 Numerical results
We evaluate our algorithm on two complementary problem classes: a synthetic suite of CUT-
GEN1 one-dimensional cutting-stock instances, which lets us generate many reproducible
problems via parametric sweeps [GW95]; and (ii) a curated set of pure-integer ILPs from MI-
PLIB 2017, providing diverse, real-world benchmarks [GHG+21]. This pairing allows us to test
behavior both in controlled settings (generation at scale) and across accepted benchmark mod-
els. Because the group relaxation is defined for any ILP and yields bounds stronger than the
plain LP relaxation, with the minimization chainOPT LP ÔëĄOPT B ÔëĄOPT, these two families
are natural testbeds.

B A Gibbs sampling approach for quantum speedup
In this section, we present a Gibbs sampler that comes from applying a Metropolis filter (i.e.
Metropolis-Hastings algorithm) to either of the Cayley walks described in Sections 4.2 and 4.3.
While Gibbs sampling for inverse-temperature╬▓>0 will outperform the uniform sam-
pling approaches, the complexity of classical Gibbs sampling from the null spaceKfor╬▓>0
is unclear. The reason we still mention the Gibbs sampler, is that if one shows that classical
Gibbs sampling at some╬▓>0 can be done classically inO(poly(n))time and the conditions
we present are met, then there is a super-quadratic speedup over the Gibbs sampler.
Beyond uniform sampling, one can also introduce a Metropolis filter (run the Metropolis-
Hastings algorithm [LP17]) to perform Gibbs sampling over the feasible coset ╦ćx+Kusing a
walk with the corresponding Gibbs measure as its stationary distribution. The Gibbs distribu-
tion over ╦ćx+Kat inverse-temperature╬▓is
¤Ç╬▓(x)ÔłŁexp (╬▓ÔčĘc,xÔčę ) ,xÔłł ╦ćx+K,
for╬▓>0. The Metropolis filter performs an accept-reject step on top of another walk, referred
to as thebase chain. Specifically, we can use either the expander or product cycle walk. One
43
step of the Metropolis-adjusted chain is as follows: starting fromx, accept a move toy╠Ş=x
proposed by the base chain with probability min (1, exp(╬▓ÔčĘc,yÔłĺxÔčę )).
For╬▓>0, the Gibbs sampler will provide a strict improvement over uniform sampling.
Unfortunately, it appears there is not much known about the complexity of sampling for╬▓>0,
implying that the current best theoretical result only applies to╬▓=0.
The below theorem shows that if there is a╬▓>0 such that the classical Metropolis chain
satisfies a LSI with sufficiently large constant, then SP can provide a super-quadratic speedup
over the Gibbs sampler. The next result follows from Theorem 2 and Corollary 1.
Theorem 12(Conditional Super-Quadratic Speedup Over Gibbs Sampling).Suppose there exists
a╬▓>0such that a Metropolis-adjusted Cayley Walk with stationary distribution¤Ç ╬▓ has LS constant
lower bounded by¤ë=ÔäŽ

for╬▒>0.
Note that the proof is effectively the same as Theorem 9 , as adding the Metropolis filter
can only decreaseÔłć P, so the choices made forÔłć P in the proof of Theorem 9 remain valid. In
addition, the Metropolis-adjusted Cayley walk can be performed with a constant number of
queries to the base chain and cost function operators using the approach of [LHP +20] com-
bined with the Cayley-walk implementation previously mentioned and Section 4.1. The above
theorem implies that if there exists a╬▓>0 that satisfies the specified conditions, the run-
time of both classical and quantum algorithms proposed in the main text can be improved as
the distribution is tilted towards the optimal solutions. Unfortunately, for most general Gibbs
measures, the distribution starts forming clusters and there is no local Markov chain that can
efficiently sample from such a measure due to long range correlations.
